command-measure-desc: Show or set the display unit system (metric/imperial)
command-steam-desc: Show or set the steam-machine recipe cost level (normal/cheap). Per-recipe numbers are in ppex_recipes.json; takes effect on the next world reload.
command-steam-retain: Steam recipe costs are already at {0}.
command-steam-set: Steam recipe costs set to {0}. Reload the world to apply.
blockdesc-pipe-bend*: A 90° bend in a pipe run.
blockdesc-pipe-passthroughbend-*: A bent pipe set in masonry.
blockdesc-pipe-outlet*: Connects a pipe run to a machine's port face.
blockdesc-pipe-fluidintake-*: Draws fresh water into the pipe network.
blockdesc-boilercornish-*: Normal single-flue boiler for low steam production.
blockdesc-boilerlancashire-*: Larger twin-flue boiler for high steam production.
blockdesc-enginecornish-*: High-pressure more effective beam engine that's steam intake can be throttled up or down. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginewatt-*: Low-pressure beam engine - the first step into steam power. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginefluidpump-*: Engine-driven pump. Transfers water from intake line to pressurised output line.
blockdesc-enginempgenerator-*: Engine-driven crank that turns axles, supplying mechanical power to machines.
blockdesc-manualfluidpump-*: Hand-cranked pump. Hold right-click to draw water from an intake.
blockdesc-steamcondenser-*: Condenses steam from its steam line back into water on its water line.
chimney-info-idle: Capping pipe network (no gas to vent)
boiler-info-needswater: Needs at least {0} of water to start boiling
engine-throttle-max: The control rods are already wide open (High).
engine-throttle-min: The control rods are already fully closed (Low).
engine-repair-wrench: Hold a wrench to repair the engine.
structure-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
game:placefailure-ppex-fluidintake-nowat: The fluid intake must be placed directly on top of a water block.
blockhelp-boiler-lid: Hold to open or close the lid
handbook-steampower-text:
Pipes and Power Expanded lets you generate mechanical power from nothing more than boiling water, by laying out pipe networks and bolting together modular steam machinery. It is powerful but demanding: the materials are expensive and a mismanaged boiler can end in a catastrophic explosion, so it is best attempted once you are well established and into the iron age. Everything you build follows one chain - a water supply feeds a boiler, the boiler drives an engine, and the engine turns a single sub-machine - and your goal is to close that chain into a loop that feeds itself while it burns coal. This article explains the pipes that tie it all together; the machines themselves are covered in Steam Power: Boilers, Steam Power: Engines and Steam Power: Fittings.

Any pipes you connect together form a single network that shares one pool. A network carries only one medium at a time - either a gas (air, exhaust or steam) or water, never both at once - and every pipe contributes 30 L of capacity to the shared total. The pressure you see, measured in atmospheres (atm), is simply the stored volume divided by that capacity, and the whole network also settles to a single temperature.

The material a pipe is made of decides how much pressure it can hold: an iron pipe bursts above 5 atm while a stronger steel pipe holds up to 10 atm, and because the weakest pipe sets the limit for the whole run, a single iron section caps an otherwise steel line. An over-pressured run survives for a short while before its pipes begin to fail.

Open ends are the other thing to watch. Any pipe connector left unconnected bleeds the network - roughly 8 L/s of gas or 10 L/s of water - so a leaking run can never build pressure beyond 1 atm, and you should always cap a run against a machine port, a valve or an outlet. Even when sealed, standing water slowly evaporates from pipes and boilers at about 50 L per day.

Laying pipe is mostly automatic: straights, bends and T- and X-junctions snap to face their neighbours as you place them, and if a piece guesses wrong you can spin it with a wrench. To feed a machine, butt a pipe directly against its port face - the boiler outlets, the engine inlets, and the pump and intake flanges.

Two client-side .exmod commands help while you build. .exmod network hi tints every networked block, giving each separate network its own translucent colour so you can see at a glance which pipes share a run and where a connection has broken, and it updates live as you work; .exmod network unhi turns it back off. .exmod measure reports your current display units, and .exmod measure metric or .exmod measure imperial switches between litres, atmospheres and degrees Celsius and gallons, psi and degrees Fahrenheit - a change to the display only, with no effect on the simulation.
handbook-boilers-text:
A boiler burns coal beside a water vessel and forces the resulting steam into a pipe network; every litre of water boils into 16 L of steam at 160-220 °C, which builds up inside the boiler and across the steam network attached to it. To build one, place the boiler block and right-click it (rightmouse) repeatedly with the required materials in your hotbar to raise it through its construction stages. It also needs a masonry firebox around its base; hold ctrl + shift + rightmouse to show the build outline, and the chat will tell you which blocks are still missing.

The Cornish Boiler is the one to start with - a simple single-flue boiler that supplies low-pressure steam, enough to get your first machines turning. Raising it consumes 24 metal plates, 18 nails-and-strips and 8 rods (iron or steel) together with 48 fire bricks, and its firebox is walled from 33 fire-brick blocks fitted with a coke-oven door, two fire-brick pipe passthroughs, a passthrough bend and a pipe outlet (both facing up), leaving one slot open for the coal pile. It pushes up to 32 L/s of steam and chokes at 5 atm; its vessel holds 800 L but needs at least 150 L present before it will boil.

The larger Lancashire Boiler is a twin-flue design that delivers higher pressures and greater volumes for the more demanding machines, though be ready for the steel it costs. It takes 36 steel plates, 24 nails-and-strips and 12 rods with 64 fire bricks, and its firebox uses 39 fire-brick blocks plus the same door, passthroughs and outlet as the Cornish. It reaches up to 48 L/s and 12 atm, with a 1200 L vessel that needs 200 L to begin boiling.

Getting a boiler running is a manual affair at first. You prime it by hand with buckets of water, and once it is making steam that steam can drive the engine behind a fluid pump, which then refills the vessel automatically - the trick is to reach the pressure your engine needs before the hand-poured water runs dry. Once that loop catches, the pumping engine keeps your boilers topped up for as long as the coal burns. In practice you hold rightmouse on the lid to open it, pour in water - from buckets, or piped under pressure into the intake from a fluid pump - and close the lid again; then you lay a coal pile in the firebox slot behind the door and set it alight, and after about three minutes of heating the boiler starts to boil and sends steam out of its top outlet toward your engines. All the while the firebox must breathe: vent its exhaust outlet through a chimney or pipe, because if the back-pressure there climbs above ~0.8 atm the fire goes out.

Boilers are dangerous. One that keeps firing with nowhere to send its steam sits at its choke pressure and can explode. Your emergency release is the lid - opening it vents steam harmlessly at around 100 L/s. An unpiped steam outlet merely leaks, which is not enough to save an over-pressured boiler, and feeding pressurised water into a boiling vessel flashes it to extra steam and spikes the pressure, so always throttle or gate any pumped feedwater. Beware in particular that a Cornish boiler's 5 atm ceiling sits above the Watt engine's 4 atm breaking point: put a pressure valve between them, with an overflow pipe, to keep the engine safe.
handbook-engines-text:
An engine draws steam from a pipe network and uses it to drive a single attached sub-machine. Place the engine first - it rises through right-click construction stages just like a boiler - then set its sub-machine in the cell directly beneath the beam's pumping rod, where it snaps itself into the correct facing.

The Watt Engine runs on large volumes of low-pressure steam. It is cheap enough to be your first steam engine, and a single Cornish boiler can power one safely. It wants 2-4 atm of inlet steam - above 4 atm it wears quickly and can break - and at that pressure it draws 30 L/s of steam and delivers 0.3 units of power (an internal value, enough to run two helve hammers without slowing), while dribbling about 1 L/s of hot condensate from its drain that you can pipe away to reclaim or leave to spill. Building it through its stages takes 4 metal plates, 24 rods and 12 nails-and-strips, all iron or steel, plus 36 fire bricks.

The Cornish Engine is the more advanced design, working at higher pressure and wringing the same power from less steam because it can regulate how much steam enters the cylinder. Running at 6-8 atm, its control rod sets a working rate of Low, Normal or High that draws 8, 16 or 32 L/s of steam and delivers 0.2, 0.4 or 0.8 units of power respectively (again an internal value); on High it will comfortably drive six helve hammers, and that same setting also scales how much water an attached pump moves. Its construction stages cost the same materials as the Watt engine - the difference in price lies in the engine's main block.

A sub-machine is the block that actually spends the engine's power (one per engine, seated in its drive cell); the engine only provides the power, so what gets done depends on which sub-machine you fit. The Mechanical Power Generator converts that power into turning force for axles, holding a steady speed as long as the load stays within the engine's rated output and overstressing to a halt only if the connected network drags it past roughly double that load. The Fluid Pump moves water from its intake line to its output line at about 5 L/s on a fully-powered engine, pressurising the output to roughly 75% of the engine's inlet steam pressure - enough to lift water into a boiler - and moving twice as much when driven by an overclocked Cornish engine. The Air Blower (from Steelmaking Expanded) instead pressurises air for the blast furnace and Bessemer converter, again up to about 75% of the inlet steam pressure, delivering roughly 16 L/s on a normally-running engine and double that from a pressured-up Cornish.

Pushed beyond its pressure band for too long, an engine breaks: the cylinder bursts and the piston is damaged. Right-click it (rightmouse) with a wrench in hand to see the repair bill, then again with those materials in your hotbar to mend it.
handbook-fittings-text:
This article gathers the smaller blocks that shape and control a pipe network.

A Valve is a hand-operated shut-off in a line. Right-clicking it (rightmouse) toggles it: while open the line flows through, and while closed the network is severed at the valve so the two sides act independently. It sits flush against a machine port.

A Pressure Valve is a one-way overflow. It holds its input side back until the pressure there rises above its gate pressure, then lets the excess spill through to its output side; a wrench flips its direction, and right-clicking it (rightmouse, or ctrl + rightmouse) raises or lowers the gate. With nothing piped to its output it simply vents the excess like a leak. It is the essential regulator between a strong boiler and a weaker engine, and its input side is marked by a copper-textured ring.

A Steam Condenser turns spent steam back into water for reuse. It pulls up to 30 L/s of steam from its steam port and adds the condensed water - one litre for every sixteen litres of steam - to a separate water line that runs through it, letting you reclaim boiler feedwater and relieve steam pressure at the same time.

A Fluid Intake draws fresh water from open water into the network. It must sit directly on a full cube of water at least three blocks deep, and it refuses to work within six blocks of another intake; feed its output into a fluid pump to lift the water onward.

The Manual Fluid Pump is a hand-cranked pump that needs no engine - look for the crank support in its model, as the side it sits on is the input side. Hold rightmouse to crank it, and as long as a working fluid intake feeds its input line it pushes about 2 L/s of water into the output at up to 1 atm. It is the way to prime a boiler's vessel before you have any steam power, and to keep that vessel topped up while pressure builds for the engines.

Finally, passthroughs and outlets are masonry blocks cast around a length of pipe, so the walls of a structure - boiler fireboxes and furnaces - can be built straight across a pipe run. An outlet ends a run at a machine's port face; or, capped with an ordinary chimney stood upright on top of it, it instead vents the network's gas to the sky at about 16 L/s - the standard way to exhaust a firebox.
handbook-steampowerguide-text:
This is a quick walkthrough of the smallest self-sufficient steam line.

Begin with water: place a Fluid Intake on a pond, on a full three-deep block of water beneath it. Next comes the boiler - build a Cornish Boiler with its firebox, fill it with water by bucket or with a Manual Fluid Pump, light a coal pile behind its door, and vent the firebox outlet through a chimney. Then add the engine: raise a Watt Engine nearby and pipe the boiler's steam outlet to its inlet through a Pressure Valve gated at around 2-3 atm, since the Cornish boiler can out-pressure the Watt.

The last piece is the sub-machine in the engine's drive cell. If you drop in a Fluid Pump, pipe the fluid intake into its intake side and run its output up into the boiler, and the water loop closes on itself; if you drop in a Mechanical Power Generator instead, hook its axles up to your helve hammers or other machines. Run the engine's condensate drain back toward the boiler whenever you are short of water, and the result is a steam plant that feeds itself.

From this foundation you can chain on more boilers and engines for greater power, or move up to a Lancashire Boiler feeding a Cornish Engine. The best use for steam power right now is to drive the machines behind Steelmaking Expanded - the air blowers of the blast furnace and the Bessemer converter - so you can turn out the iron and steel your industrial revolution will demand.
